volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Little Carthaginian (Section 5.4.1243b - Section 5.5.1319)

sections.label: Section 5.4.1243b
sections.translation: But , i ’ faith , I ’ ve got something to do with you ; for he is my kinsman ; it ’ s necessary for me to be his advocate .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1245
sections.original: et praedicabo quo modo vos furta faciatis multa
sections.translation: And I ’ ll inform him how you are guilty of many a theft , and in what way you have got his daughters as slaves at your house , whom you know to be free women stolen from their native land .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1248
sections.original: Vbi sunt eae ? aut quas , opsecro ?
sections.translation: Where are these , or who are they , prithee ?

sections.label: Section 5.4.1248b
sections.translation: They have been teased sufficiently .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1249
sections.translation: Why not speak out , then ?

sections.label: Section 5.4.1249b
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ m of that opinion , kinsman .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1249c
sections.translation: I ’ m dreadfully afraid

sections.label: Section 5.4.1250
sections.original: quid hoc sít negoti , mea soror ; ita stupida sine animo asto .
sections.translation: what this business can mean , my sister ; so astounded am I , I stand here without my senses .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1251
sections.original: Advortite animum , mulieres . primum , si id fieri possit ,
sections.translation: Damsels , give me your attention . In the first place , if it could possibly come to pass , for the Gods not to send upon the innocent what is undeserved , that could I have wished to happen ; now for the good the Gods bestow upon me , upon yourselves and upon your nurse [*] , ’ tis due that we should give to the Deities our endless thanks ,

sections.label: Section 5.4.1255
sections.original: quom nostram pietatem adprobant decorantque di immortales .
sections.translation: since the immortal Gods approve and reward our piety . You are my daughters , both of you , and this is your relation , Agorastocles , the son of my cousin .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1257b
sections.translation: Prithee , are they deluding us with imaginary joys ?

sections.label: Section 5.4.1258b
sections.translation: Really , so may the Deities preserve me , this is your father . Give him your hands .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1259b
sections.translation: Welcome , father ! unhoped-for by us , allow us to embrace you !

sections.label: Section 5.4.1260b
sections.translation: Welcome , father ! much wished and longed for !

sections.label: Section 5.4.1261b
sections.translation: We are both your daughters ;

sections.label: Section 5.4.1261c
sections.translation: we both embrace you .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1262
sections.translation: Who ’ ll be for embracing me in the next place ?

sections.label: Section 5.4.1262b
sections.translation: Now am I happy ! Now with this delight do I allay the miseries of many a year .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1264
sections.translation: We hardly seem to believe this .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1264b
sections.translation: I ’ ll tell you something to make you believe it the more :

sections.label: Section 5.4.1265
sections.original: nam vostra nutrix † primum me cognovit .
sections.translation: why , it was your nurse who recognized me first .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1265b
sections.translation: Prithee , where is she ?

sections.label: Section 5.4.1266
sections.translation: She ’ s at his house .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1266b
sections.original: Quaeso , qui lubet tam diu tenere collum ?
sections.translation: Pray , why does it please you to clasp his neck so long ,

sections.label: Section 5.4.1267
sections.original: omitte saltem tu altera . nolo ego istuc .
sections.translation: Do leave off your salutations !

sections.label: Section 5.4.1267b
sections.translation: I don ’ t want that ; [*] you torment me to death !

sections.label: Section 5.4.1267a
sections.translation: And you the other one . [*]

sections.label: Section 5.4.1268
sections.translation: before he has betrothed you to me ?

sections.label: Section 5.4.1268b
sections.translation: I will leave off .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1268c
sections.translation: Dear one , much longed-for , blessings on you !

sections.label: Section 5.4.1269
sections.original: Condamus alter alterum ergo in nervom bracchialem .
sections.translation: Let us each clasp the other in our arms ,

sections.label: Section 5.4.1270
sections.original: quibus núnc in terra melius est ?
sections.translation: than whom is there anything on earth more happy ?

sections.label: Section 5.4.1270b
sections.translation: Blessings befall the deserving .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1271
sections.translation: ( Pointing to HANNO .) At last his wishes are realized !

sections.label: Section 5.4.1271b
sections.translation: O Apelles ! O Zeuxis [*] the painter ! why did you die too soon ? Would that you could paint a subject after this ! For I don ’ t care for other common painters to be treating subjects of this description .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1274
sections.original: Dí deaeque omnes , vóbis habeo mérito magnas grátias ,
sections.translation: Gods and Goddesses all ! I return you deservedly extreme thanks ,

sections.label: Section 5.4.1275
sections.original: quom hac me laetitia adfecistis tanta et tantis gaudiis ,
sections.translation: for having blest me with this gladness so supreme and with these joys so great ; as my daughters have returned to me and into my possession .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1277
sections.original: Mi pater , tua pietas plane nobis auxilio fuit .
sections.translation: My father , your own piety has clearly come to our aid .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1278
sections.original: Patrue , facito in memoria habeas , tuam maiorem filiam
sections.translation: Kinsman , take care and keep it in memory that you ’ ve betrothed your elder daughter to me —

sections.label: Section 5.4.1279b
sections.translation: I remember it .

sections.label: Section 5.4.1279c
sections.translation: The portion , too , that you promised .

sections.label: Section 5.5.1280
sections.original: Si ego minam non ultus fuero probe , quam lenoni dedi ,
sections.translation: If I don ’ t take full revenge for that mina which I gave to the Procurer , then really may the townspeople make a butt of me ! This most rascally fellow even brought me to his house to breakfast . He himself went away out of doors , and left me as his chamberlain [*] in the house . When neither the Procurer nor these women came back , nor anything was given me to eat ,

sections.label: Section 5.5.1285
sections.original: pro maiore parte prandi pignus cepi , abii foras ;
sections.translation: for the best part of the breakfast I took a pledge [*] , and came out of doors . This way I ’ ll pay him . I ’ ll touch up the rascally Procurer in the military way of payment [*] . He did get hold of a person for him to bamboozle out of a mina of silver ! But I wish that my mistress would now come in my way while thus enraged . Then , by my troth , with my fists I ’ d make her quite black all over ;

sections.label: Section 5.5.1290
sections.original: ita replebo átritate , átrior multo ut siet ,
sections.translation: I ’ d cover her so with swarthiness , that she should be much more swarthy than the Egyptians , or than those who carry the buckets [*] at the games in the Circus .

sections.label: Section 5.5.1292
sections.original: Tene sis me arte , mea voluptas ; male ego metuo miluos ,
sections.translation: Do hold me fast , please , my love ; I sadly fear the kites ; this is an evil animal — lest perchance he may carry me off , your chick .

sections.label: Section 5.5.1294
sections.original: Vt nequeo te satis complecti , mi pater .
sections.translation: I cannot clasp you fast enough , my father !

sections.label: Section 5.5.1294b
sections.translation: I ’ m delaying .

sections.label: Section 5.5.1295
sections.original: propemodúm hoc opsonare prandium potero mihi .
sections.translation: ( Looking in his hand .) I can now pretty nearly cater a breakfast for myself with this . ( Raising his eyes .) But what ’ s this ? How ’ s this ? What ’ s this ? What ’ s this I see ? How now ? What means this strange conjunction ? What ’ s this coupling together ? Who ’ s this fellow with the long skirts , just like a tavern-boy ? Do I quite see with my eyes ? Isn ’ t this my mistress , Anterastylis ?

sections.label: Section 5.5.1300
sections.original: et ea est certo . iam pridem ego me sensi nihili pendier .
sections.translation: Why , surely it is she . For some time past I ’ ve perceived that I ’ m set at nought . Isn ’ t the girl ashamed to be hugging a tawny fellow in the middle of the street ? I faith , I shall give him up forthwith to the executioner to be tortured all over . Surely this is a womanish race [*] , with their tunics hanging down to their heels . But I ’ m determined to accost this African female lover .

sections.label: Section 5.5.1305
sections.original: heus tu , tibi dico , mulier , ecquid te pudet ?
sections.translation: ( To HANNO .) Hallo ! you woman , I say , are you not ashamed ? What business have you with her , pray ? Tell me .

sections.label: Section 5.5.1307
sections.translation: Young man , greetings to you .

sections.label: Section 5.5.1307b
sections.translation: I don ’ t want them ; that ’ s nothing to you . What business have you to touch her with a finger ?

sections.label: Section 5.5.1308b
sections.translation: Because I choose .

sections.label: Section 5.5.1309
sections.translation: You choose ?

sections.label: Section 5.5.1309b
sections.translation: I say so .

sections.label: Section 5.5.1309c
sections.translation: Away to utter perdition , you shoe-latchet !

sections.label: Section 5.5.1310
sections.original: tune hic amator audes esse , hallex viri ,
sections.translation: What , do you dare to be acting the lover here , you great toe of a man [*] , or to be meddling with an object which masculine men are fond of , you skinnea pilchard , you deformed image of Serapis [*] , you half-apron , you sheepskin-jacket [*] , you pot of stinking sea-salt ; more crammed , too , to boot , with leeks and garlick than the Roman rowers ?

sections.label: Section 5.5.1315
sections.original: Num tibi , adulescens , malae aut dentes pruriunt ,
sections.translation: Young man , do your jaws or your teeth itch , that you are annoying this person , or are you in search of a heavy mishap ?

sections.label: Section 5.5.1317
sections.original: Quin adhibuisti , dum istaec loquere , tympanum ?
sections.translation: Why didn ’ t you use a drum [*] while you were saying that ? For I take you to be more of an effeminate wretch than a real man .

sections.label: Section 5.5.1319
sections.original: Scin quam cinaedus sum ? ite istinc , servi , foras ,
sections.translation: Do you understand what sort of effeminate wretch I am ? ( Calling aloud .) Servants , come out of doors ,
